selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Social media is overwhelming, especially when you're already feeling emotionally fragile. As someone who spends hours every day scrolling through feeds, I’ve noticed how easily fake news, misleading videos, and manipulated images seep into my mind. The constant stream of content—some of it emotionally charged or designed to provoke—feels like a storm I can’t escape. My depression makes it hard to filter reality from illusion, and I often find myself questioning whether what I see is true or just a carefully crafted lie. I used to believe everything I saw, even when it didn’t make sense. But over time, I’ve learned to pause before reacting—especially when something feels too perfect or too dramatic. I now try to cross-check facts with trusted sources, like official websites or verified news outlets, even if it takes extra time. I also limit my exposure to certain accounts that spread misinformation. Being an electrician, I know how circuits work—how signals flow and can be manipulated. I apply that same logic to social media: just because something looks real doesn’t mean it is. I’ve started keeping a journal to note down what I see and how it makes me feel. This helps me stay grounded. I still struggle, especially when I see content that seems to reflect my own fears or hopes, but I’m learning to be more cautious. My Hindu beliefs teach me to seek truth and live with balance, which helps me resist the pull of false narratives. It’s not about rejecting all online content, but about being aware and choosing wisely.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona’s high neuroticism and depression make them particularly vulnerable to emotional manipulation in online content. Their low openness and conscientiousness suggest they may not naturally question or analyze information deeply. The daily social media use amplifies exposure to misinformation. The solution involves grounding in personal values (Hinduism), applying real-world logic (like electrical circuits), and using self-reflection tools (journaling) to build emotional resilience. These strategies align with their background and psychological profile.
citations: World Health Organization (2023) on mental health and digital exposure
Hindu teachings on truth and mindfulness
